title: Rooted: Stories of Entrepreneurship in Agribusiness
text:
Welcome to Rooted: Stories of Entrepreneurship in Agribusiness, a podcast from Colorado State University’s Department of Agricultural and Resource Economics.
In this podcast series, we explore stories of how people across the food and agriculture industry identify problems, create solutions, and build or improve businesses that make an impact.
What makes Rooted unique is that each episode is co-hosted by students in the Agribusiness Entrepreneurship course, who bring their own curiosity and questions to the table.
Their conversations with leaders across food and agriculture are a chance to learn, reflect, and share those insights with you, our audience of students, entrepreneurs, and changemakers in food and agriculture.
I appreciate your time and attention, and I’m excited to share these inspiring stories with you!

Playlist coming soon!

title: Lauren Chenarides
text:
Welcome to my personal website!
I am a researcher, economist, data geek, baker, dog mom, and Assistant Professor at Colorado State University. I study food retail markets and how market structure impacts consumer food decisions. Through projects like DemocratizingData.ai, I work to make information more transparent, equitable, and built to serve the public good.

title: Colorado Center for Food Innovation
text:
The Colorado Center for Food Innovation (CCFI) is a new multidisciplinary consortium working to remove barriers that prevent next-generation food products from reaching the market.
We integrate data-driven research, targeted policy advocacy, and high-impact industry partnerships to ensure innovations translate directly from the lab to commercial and widespread home adoption.

title: Colorado Center for Food Innovation
text:
The Colorado Center for Food Innovation (CCFI) is a new multidisciplinary consortium working to remove barriers that prevent next-generation food products from reaching the market.
We integrate data-driven research, targeted policy advocacy, and high-impact industry partnerships to ensure innovations translate directly from the lab to commercial and widespread home adoption.

title: Colorado Center for Food Innovation
text:
Vision
To build a resilient food system that links farmers, entrepreneurs, and policymakers to scale sustainable innovation benefiting both producers and consumers.

title: Colorado Center for Food Innovation
text:
Who We Are
We are a multidisciplinary research consortium combining expertise in food science, engineering, and consumer economics.
Our work advances research and application to address supply chain challenges and develop scalable, evidence-based food system solutions.

title: Colorado Center for Food Innovation
text:
The Problem
Despite rapid innovation, many next-generation sustainable foods—particularly plant-based and hybrid meat alternatives—fail to achieve wide consumer adoption.
The barriers extend beyond technology to include:

Poor culinary performance in common home appliances

Psychological resistance to new foods and technologies

Gaps between policy support and market incentives

Limited technical workforce to sustain growth

title: Colorado Center for Food Innovation
text:
Strategic Goals

Solve for Consumer Compliance
Develop formulations and processing methods that ensure new foods perform flawlessly in home appliances (microwaves, air fryers) to drive repeat purchases.
Bridge the Policy-to-Market Gap
Generate actionable, data-driven policy and economic insights to unlock state funding, expand market access, and remove legislative and financial barriers that limit food innovation.
Accelerate High-Growth Markets
Focus research and industry partnerships on urgent, high-value opportunities, such as high-protein foods for the GLP-1 consumer segment.
Build a Skilled Workforce
Create specialized training programs that address the technical and analytical needs of rapidly scaling next-generation food companies.

title: Colorado Center for Food Innovation
text:
The Solution
Integrated, Cross-Disciplinary Research
By combining methods and perspectives from food science, engineering, and consumer economics, we develop comprehensive solutions that are both innovative and immediately applicable.
Our work translates innovations from the lab to commercial production and widespread home adoption.

title: Colorado Center for Food Innovation
text:
The Impact
Our interdisciplinary research drives workforce development, fosters economic growth, and strengthens the food system through measurable consumer compliance (repeat purchases) and evidence-based solutions.

title: Colorado Center for Food Innovation
text:
Why Us
Our track record of collaboration, interdisciplinary expertise, and focus on practical outcomes make us a trusted partner in advancing a more resilient and innovative food system.

title: Colorado Center for Food Innovation
text:
Why You
Your partnership helps translate research into real-world impact.
Through funding, collaboration, and community engagement, you help advance workforce development, foster economic innovation, and strengthen the resilience of the food system.

title: Colorado Center for Food Innovation
text: Affiliates

title: Colorado Center for Food Innovation

Contact
📍 Colorado State University, Fort Collins, CO
📧 Lauren.Chenarides@colostate.edu

title: About Lauren
text:
Welcome! I am a researcher, economist, data geek, baker, dog mom, and Assistant Professor at Colorado State University. I study food retail markets and how market structure impacts consumer food decisions. Through projects like DemocratizingData.ai, I work to make information more transparent, equitable, and built to serve the public good. See my CV for more details.

Short Bio
Lauren Chenarides is an Assistant Professor at Colorado State University in the Department of Agricultural and Resource Economics. Her research focuses on how food retail markets and consumer food decisions interact and contribute to economic outcomes across communities. She also studies the role of data infrastructure in supporting evidence-based food policy, including efforts to increase transparency and public access to high-quality datasets through initiatives like DemocratizingData.ai. Lauren has expertise in applied microeconomics, industrial organization, spatial analysis, scanner data, and food systems research. She regularly works with large-scale retail and household datasets and collaborates with interdisciplinary teams on policy-relevant research. She holds a Ph.D. in Agricultural and Resource Economics from Penn State and a B.A. in Mathematics from the College of the Holy Cross.


Looking for a speaker, advisor, or collaborator? Let’s talk.

title: Build Your Academic Website with Quarto and GitHub
text:
By the end of this lab, you will have a starter academic website that lives in your own GitHub repository and can be published online with GitHub Pages.
We will use Professor Chenarides’ website repository as the starting point:
https://github.com/laurenchenarides/website
You will make your own copy, edit the pages in RStudio, render the website with Quarto, and push your changes to GitHub.






NoteWhat you are learning





This lab is not just about making a website. You are learning a basic research workflow:

Keep project files organized in a folder.
Track changes with Git.
Store and share your project on GitHub.
Use Quarto to turn plain text files into a polished website.
Publish the site using GitHub Pages.

title: Build Your Academic Website with Quarto and GitHub
text:
By the end of this lab, you will have a starter academic website that lives in your own GitHub repository and can be published online with GitHub Pages.
We will use Professor Chenarides’ website repository as the starting point:
https://github.com/laurenchenarides/website
You will make your own copy, edit the pages in RStudio, render the website with Quarto, and push your changes to GitHub.






NoteWhat you are learning





This lab is not just about making a website. You are learning a basic research workflow:

Keep project files organized in a folder.
Track changes with Git.
Store and share your project on GitHub.
Use Quarto to turn plain text files into a polished website.
Publish the site using GitHub Pages.

title: Build Your Academic Website with Quarto and GitHub
text:
The tools
Before we start, it helps to separate the tools.








Tool
What it does
How we use it today




RStudio
The program where we edit files and run commands
We will open the website project in RStudio


Quarto
Turns .qmd files into web pages
We will render the website


Git
Tracks changes to files over time
We will commit changes


GitHub
Stores your Git project online
We will push the site to GitHub


GitHub Pages
Publishes a GitHub repository as a website
We will make the site public online



A simple way to remember this:

RStudio is where you work.
Git tracks your work.
GitHub stores your work online.
Quarto turns your writing into a website.
GitHub Pages publishes the website.

title: Build Your Academic Website with Quarto and GitHub
text:
The website structure
The starter repository is a Quarto website. The main files include:







File or folder
What it does




_quarto.yml
Controls the website title, navigation menu, theme, and publishing settings


index.qmd
The homepage


bio.qmd
The About page


cv.qmd
The CV page


images/
Stores photos and other images


docs/
Stores the rendered website files that GitHub Pages publishes


website.Rproj
Opens the project in RStudio



The most important file for the website structure is _quarto.yml.
In this repository, the website is configured to render into the docs/ folder:
project:
  type: website
  output-dir: docs
That matters because GitHub Pages can be told to publish the site from the docs/ folder on the main branch.

title: Build Your Academic Website with Quarto and GitHub
text:
Instructor setup
Before students begin, the instructor should decide whether students will use a template repository or a fork.
Recommended option: use a template repository
A template repository is usually the cleanest option for teaching. Students get their own copy of the files without preserving the full commit history of the original website.
To make the repository a template:

Go to the repository on GitHub.
Click Settings.
Scroll to Template repository.
Check the box.

Then students can click Use this template and create their own copy.
Backup option: fork the repository
If the repository is not set as a template, students can click Fork.
A fork also creates a copy, but it keeps a visible connection to the original repository. That is fine for this workshop, but a template is a little easier to explain.






TipTeaching recommendation



For this lab, I recommend using Use this template if possible.
Say this to students:

We are using my website as a starter template. You are not editing my website. You are creating your own copy and then customizing it.

title: Build Your Academic Website with Quarto and GitHub
text:
Student setup

Create a GitHub account
Go to https://github.com.
Click Sign up.
Choose:

a username
an email address
a password

Your username matters because it becomes part of your website address.
For example, if your username is janedoe, your default GitHub Pages website can look like one of these:
https://janedoe.github.io
https://janedoe.github.io/repository-name






WarningPick a professional username





For an academic website, choose a username you would be comfortable putting on your CV, job market materials, or research projects.





Install the software
You need four things on your computer:

R
RStudio
Quarto
Git

Download them here:



Software
Link




R
https://cran.r-project.org/


RStudio Desktop
https://posit.co/download/rstudio-desktop/


Quarto
https://quarto.org/docs/get-started/


Git
https://git-scm.com/downloads









NoteHow do I know whether Git is installed?





Open RStudio.
Go to:
Tools > Global Options > Git/SVN
If RStudio sees Git, you should see a path to a Git executable.
On Windows, it may look something like:
C:/Program Files/Git/bin/git.exe

title: Build Your Academic Website with Quarto and GitHub
text:
Create your website repository
Go to the starter repository:
https://github.com/laurenchenarides/website
Option A: If you see “Use this template”
Click Use this template.
Choose Create a new repository.
Use one of these repository names:



Repository name
Resulting website address




username.github.io
https://username.github.io


website
https://username.github.io/website



For a personal academic website, I recommend:
username.github.io
Replace username with your actual GitHub username.
For example:
janedoe.github.io
Choose Public.
Click Create repository.
Option B: If you see “Fork”
Click Fork.
Change the repository name if needed.
For a personal academic website, use:
username.github.io
Choose Public.
Click Create fork.






ImportantPublic vs. private



Use Public for this lab.
GitHub Pages websites are intended to be visible online. Do not put private information, sensitive data, unpublished datasets, passwords, API keys, or confidential files in this repository.

title: Build Your Academic Website with Quarto and GitHub
text:
Open the project in RStudio
You need a way for RStudio to talk to GitHub.
The easiest beginner-friendly workflow is:

Use GitHub to create the repository.
Use RStudio to clone the repository.
Use RStudio’s Git tab to commit and push changes.

Copy your repository URL
Go to your new GitHub repository.
Click the green Code button.
Copy the HTTPS URL.
It will look like this:
https://github.com/username/username.github.io.git
or:
https://github.com/username/website.git
Clone the repository in RStudio
Open RStudio.
Go to:
File > New Project > Version Control > Git
Paste your repository URL into Repository URL.
Choose where the project should live on your computer.
Click Create Project.
RStudio will download the website files and open the project.






TipWhat does “clone” mean?





To clone a repository means to download a copy from GitHub to your computer.
After cloning, you have two connected versions:

the local version on your computer
the remote version on GitHub

When you edit files locally, you commit and push them back to GitHub.

title: Build Your Academic Website with Quarto and GitHub
text:
Edit and preview the site

Preview the website
In RStudio, open the file:
index.qmd
This is the homepage.
Now preview the website.
In RStudio, look for the Build tab.
Click:
Render Website
If that works, RStudio will render the Quarto website.
You can also run this command in the Terminal tab:
quarto render
The rendered website files will appear in the docs/ folder.






NoteSource files vs. rendered files





You edit the .qmd files.
Quarto creates the .html files.
For example:



You edit
Quarto creates




index.qmd
docs/index.html


bio.qmd
docs/bio.html


cv.qmd
docs/cv.html








Customize the homepage
Open:
index.qmd
Find the title and homepage text.
Replace the existing information with your own.
For example:
---
title: "Your Name"
image: "images/photo.png"
execute:
  echo: false
  warning: false
about:
  template: trestles
  links:
    - icon: github
      href: https://github.com/yourusername
    - icon: linkedin
      href: https://www.linkedin.com/in/yourname/
---

### Welcome!

I am a PhD student in economics. My research interests include labor economics, public economics, industrial organization, and applied econometrics.

This website includes my research, teaching, CV, and contact information.
Edit the text so that it sounds like you.
You do not need to sound overly polished. A simple first version is fine.






TipSuggested homepage structure





Use three short parts:

Who you are
What you study
What people can find on your site

Example:

I am a PhD student in economics at [University]. I study [field/topic]. This site includes my research, teaching materials, CV, and contact information.






Add or replace your photo
Put your photo in the images/ folder.
A simple filename is best:
photo.png
or:
headshot.jpg
Then update the image line in index.qmd:
image: "images/headshot.jpg"
Render the website again.
quarto render
Check that the image appears.






WarningImage filenames are picky





These are different filenames:
Headshot.jpg
headshot.jpg
headshot.JPG
Use simple lowercase filenames with no spaces.





Customize the navigation menu
Open:
_quarto.yml
Look for the section that controls the navigation bar.
It will look something like this:
website:
  title: "your-site-title"
  navbar:
    right:
      - text: "About"
        href: bio.qmd
      - text: "CV"
        href: cv.qmd
You can add, remove, or rename pages.
For a PhD student website, a good starter menu is:
website:
  title: "Your Name"
  navbar:
    right:
      - text: "About"
        href: bio.qmd
      - text: "Research"
        href: research.qmd
      - text: "Teaching"
        href: teaching.qmd
      - text: "CV"
        href: cv.qmd
If you add a page to the menu, you also need to create the matching .qmd file.
For example, if you add:
- text: "Research"
  href: research.qmd
then create a file named:
research.qmd


Create a research page
In RStudio, create a new file:
File > New File > Quarto Document
Save it as:
research.qmd
You can start with this:
---
title: "Research"
---

## Research interests

I am interested in:

- Topic 1
- Topic 2
- Topic 3

## Working papers

### Paper title

Brief description of the paper.

**Status:** In progress.

## Publications

Add publications here as they become available.
Render the website again:
quarto render

title: Build Your Academic Website with Quarto and GitHub
text:
Save and publish changes

Commit and push
Now we need to send your changes back to GitHub.
In RStudio, open the Git tab.
You should see a list of changed files.
Check the boxes next to the files you changed.
Click Commit.
Write a short commit message.
Examples:
Customize homepage
Add research page
Update CV page
Click Commit.
Then click Push.






NoteWhat is a commit?





A commit is a saved checkpoint in your project history.
A good commit message briefly says what changed.









NoteWhat is a push?





A push sends your committed changes from your computer to GitHub.





Publish with GitHub Pages
Go to your repository on GitHub.
Click Settings.
In the left menu, click Pages.
Under Build and deployment, choose:

Source: Deploy from a branch
Branch: main
Folder: /docs

Click Save.
GitHub will publish the website.
The first publication can take a few minutes.
Your site will usually appear at one of these addresses:
https://username.github.io
or:
https://username.github.io/repository-name






ImportantWhy do we choose /docs?



This Quarto website renders the finished HTML files into the docs/ folder.
GitHub Pages needs to know where the finished website files are.
For this project, the answer is:
main branch / docs folder




Check your website
After GitHub Pages finishes publishing, open your website in a browser.
Check:

Does the homepage load?
Does your photo show up?
Do the navigation links work?
Does the CV page open?
Does the research page open?
Are there any old links or names that still need to be replaced?

If something is wrong, go back to RStudio, edit the file, render again, commit, and push.
That is the basic cycle:
Edit -> Render -> Commit -> Push -> Check website

title: Build Your Academic Website with Quarto and GitHub
text:
Common errors and fixes
My website still shows the old version
Try these steps:

Wait a minute and refresh the page.
Make sure you pushed your changes.
Make sure you rendered the website before committing.
Make sure GitHub Pages is publishing from main and /docs.

My image does not show up
Check:

Is the image in the images/ folder?
Does the filename match exactly?
Did you render the website after adding the image?
Did you commit and push the image file?

My navigation link is broken
Check:

Does the .qmd file exist?
Is the filename spelled the same way in _quarto.yml?
Did you render the website after editing _quarto.yml?

RStudio asks me to log in to GitHub
This is normal.
Follow the login prompts.
If RStudio cannot authenticate, use GitHub’s instructions for connecting Git to GitHub, or ask for help during the lab.
GitHub Pages says the site is not published
Check:

Is the repository public?
Is the source set to Deploy from a branch?
Is the branch set to main?
Is the folder set to /docs?
Does the docs/ folder contain an index.html file?

title: Build Your Academic Website with Quarto and GitHub
text:
Workshop plan







Time
Activity




0–5 min
Explain Git, GitHub, Quarto, RStudio, and GitHub Pages


5–10 min
Students create or log into GitHub


10–15 min
Students create their own copy of the repository


15–20 min
Students clone the repository into RStudio


20–25 min
Students edit index.qmd, render the site, and preview it


25–30 min
Students commit, push, and configure GitHub Pages









TipGood stopping point



The minimum successful outcome is:

The student has their own repository.
The repository opens in RStudio.
The student changes the homepage.
The student commits and pushes the change.

Publishing with GitHub Pages can be completed at the end or immediately after class.

title: Build Your Academic Website with Quarto and GitHub
text:
After the workshop
After the first version is online, improve the website one page at a time.
Suggested pages:



Page
What to include




About
Short bio, research interests, education


Research
Working papers, publications, projects


Teaching
Courses taught, teaching interests


CV
Link to a PDF CV


Contact
Email, office, GitHub, Google Scholar, LinkedIn



Before sharing your website, check:

Your name is correct.
Your homepage says who you are and what you study.
Your photo loads.
Your CV link works.
Your GitHub and LinkedIn links work.
There is no placeholder text.
There are no private files in the repository.
The website works on your phone.
The site looks professional enough to share.

Optional: Add your CV
Save your CV as:
cv.pdf
Put it in the main project folder.
Then link to it from cv.qmd:
---
title: "CV"
---

You can download my CV [here](cv.pdf).
Render, commit, and push.
Optional: Use a custom domain later
You do not need a custom domain for this lab.
The free GitHub Pages address is enough to get started.
Later, you can connect a custom domain such as:
yourname.com
or:
yourname.org
For today, focus on publishing the site with the default GitHub Pages address.

title: Build Your Academic Website with Quarto and GitHub
text:
Final workflow
Every time you update your website, use the same workflow:
1. Edit the .qmd files in RStudio
2. Render the website
3. Look at the preview
4. Commit the changed files
5. Push to GitHub
6. Check the live website
That is the foundation of a reproducible website workflow.

title: Teaching
section: Building an Academic Website with GitHub and Quarto
text:
Building an Academic Website with GitHub and Quarto
In this lab, students create a personal academic website using GitHub, RStudio, and Quarto. The notes assume no prior experience with GitHub or Git.
Open lab notes
